title: Research Highlights
text:
🏆 Winner, 2026 Best PhD Dissertation Award
Transportation Geography Specialty Group, American Association of Geographers
🔗 Reproducible and Open dissertation repository (PDF, code, figures):
https://github.com/soukhova/Dissertation

My monograph-style dissertation reconnects accessibility measures with their theoretical roots in spatial interaction modelling.
I argue that widely used Hansen-type accessibility measures (Hansen, 1959) were originally grounded in spatial interaction theory, but have since drifted away from those foundations. This work reunites them by introducing proportional allocation mechanisms, inspired by the balancing factors of spatial interaction models.
What does this achieve?
Accessibility measures with consistent, interpretable units, and a unified framework that integrates traditional, competitive, and interaction-based approaches.




Introduces a family of accessibility measures grounded in spatial interaction principles
Clarifies the distinction between potential accessibility and realized interaction
Restores meaningful units (e.g., accessible opportunities or population) to accessibility metrics
Demonstrates how constrained measures improve interpretability and comparability

The early chapters develop the theoretical framework, and the final chapters apply these ideas empirically, focusing on Parkland provision and accessibility in the City of Toronto.

While the dissertation is a stand-alone work, it is based on the following three published papers:



🔗 Paper: https://osf.io/a9dxb_v1
🔗 Repo: https://github.com/soukhova/family-accessibility-measures
This paper develops the theoretical foundation underlying the dissertation.
Rather than proposing a single accessibility metric, we introduce a family of measures derived from entropy-based spatial interaction principles. We formalize four cases:

Unconstrained (Hansen-type accessibility)
Total constrained
Singly constrained (linked to 2SFCA / competitive accessibility)
Doubly constrained (realized interaction or “access”)

Why this matters:
Although accessibility is widely used, many measures lack clear units and are difficult to interpret. By grounding accessibility in spatial interaction modelling, these measures recover meaningful quantities (e.g., reachable opportunities or accessible population) instead of abstract index values.
These measures are designed to support planners, geographers, and policy analysts who need accessibility metrics that are intuitive, comparable, and actionable. We are also integrating them into the {accessibility} R package to facilitate applied use.




🔗Spatial availability papers (PLOS ONE):
Introducing spatial availability: https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0299077
Multimodal extension: https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0278468
🔗 Repos:
https://github.com/soukhova/Spatial-Availability-Measure
https://github.com/soukhova/multimodal-spatial-availability
Spatial availability is a singly constrained competitive accessibility measure designed for contexts where opportunities are exhaustible or competitive.
In contrast to conventional (unconstrained) accessibility measures, which can double-count opportunities; spatial availability constrains accessibility values so they sum to a known total (e.g., the total number of jobs or services in a region). Each zonal value can therefore be interpreted as a proportion of total opportunities.
We extend this framework to the multimodal case, allowing accessibility to be compared meaningfully across modes.
Key empirical insight (Madrid case study):

Outside the city centre, car users have higher spatial availability due to greater travel ranges
Within the city centre (and especially inside the Low Emission Zone) non-car modes exhibit higher spatial availability
These patterns align closely with policy design and lived travel constraints

Multimodal spatial availability is particularly useful when analysts want to:

Compare accessibility across modes or population groups
Avoid inflated or misleading accessibility values
Evaluate policy interventions such as Low Emission Zones

title: Home
text:
I’m Anastasia Soukhov, a PhD in Geography from McMaster University (Canada) with degrees in transportation and civil engineering.
This is a little home for comments on my research. Currently, I’m a Postdoctoral Associate at Western University in London Ontario, Canada working with the Human Environments Analysis Laboratory. My research focuses on:

Accessibility and spatial interaction modeling
Built environment and transportation
Social inequities, inequality and sustainability
Open science

Learn more About Me, my Publications, and Research Highlights.

title: About Me
text: I am a researcher in Geography, interested in sustainable urban systems, transportation, and social equity. I use R, GIS, and spatial analysis methods to explore how built environments function and how to make them more equitable.

title: About Me
text:
Academic Positions

Currently: Post Doctoral Associate, Western University, 2025-Ongoing. Research funded under the Western University PostDoctoral Fellowship Program.
Research Assistant

Mobilizing Justice, 2021–2025
Transit Research Informed Practice (TRiP) lab, McMaster University, 2019-2021 Cost-effectiveness, GHG abatement analysis, and stakeholder engagement of EV, alternative fuel, and transit decarbonization policies

Sessional Instructor: Applied Spatial Statistics, Winter 2023 (check out the Open Education Resources!)
Teaching Assistant (various GIS, Spatial Statistics, Transportation, Sustainability, etc.), 2020-2024

title: About Me
text:
Education

PhD, Geography, McMaster University, Completed: 2025
MASc, Transportation Engineering, McMaster University, Completed: 2021
BEng, Civil Engineering & Society (Min. Geographic Information Systems) with a placement in the private and public sector, McMaster University, Completed: 2020

title: Publications
text: List of first-author publications, with a short highlight, links to their Repos and PDFs:

title: Publications
text:
2025

Towards completely caring 15-minute neighbourhoods
Soukhov, A. and Léa Ravensbergen and Lucía Mejía Dorantes and Antonio Páez. Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice. Repo · Article

Extends the 15-minute neighbourhood concept by centring care needs, demonstrating how accessibility to care-related destinations reshapes planning priorities for equitable, everyday urban life.

Ten Years of School Closures and Consolidations in Hamilton, Canada and the Impact on Multimodal Accessibility
Soukhov, A. and Christopher D. Higgins and Antonio Páez and Moataz Mohamed. Networks and Spatial Economics. Repo · Article

Shows how a decade of school closures and consolidations in Hamilton systematically reduced children’s accessibility to school seats, especially by active modes. Who pays for school closures and consolidations? It reveals uneven spatial and modal impacts with important equity implications.

Standards of fairness in the transportation literature
Soukhov, A. and Ignacio Tiznado-Aitken and Matthew Palm and Steven Farber and Antonio Paez. Transportation. Repo · Article

Systematically reviews how fairness and equity are defined and operationalized in transportation research, identifying gaps, inconsistencies, and opportunities for clearer standards in equity-oriented analysis.

Exploring Mobility of Care with Measures of Accessibility
Soukhov, A. and Nicholas Mooney and Léa Ravensbergen. Journal of Transport Geography. Repo · Article

Operationalizes the concept of “mobility of care” using accessibility measures, showing how care-related trip destinations paint a different accessibility landscape than commute-focused destinations

A family of accessibility measures derived from spatial interaction principles
Soukhov, A. and Rafael H. M. Pereira and Christopher D. Higgins and Antonio Páez. PLOS One. Repo · Article

Introduces a family of accessibility measures grounded in spatial interaction modelling, recovering interpretable units and formally linking Hansen-type, competitive, and realized-access measures.

title: Publications
text:
2024

Multimodal Spatial Availability: A Singly-Constrained Measure of Accessibility Considering Multiple Modes
Soukhov, A. and Javier Tarriño-Ortiz and Julio A. Soria-Lara and Antonio Páez. PLOS ONE. Repo · Article

Extends spatial availability to multiple modes/segments; demonstrated on Madrid LEZ with interpretable, constrained accessibility.

title: Publications
text:
2023

TTS2016R: A Data Set to Study Population and Employment Patterns from the 2016 Transportation Tomorrow Survey in the Greater Golden Horseshoe Area, Ontario, Canada
Soukhov, A. and Antonio Páez. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science. Repo · Article

Open R data package for GGH commuting: TAZs, OD flows, travel times, and reproducible examples (distance decay).

Introducing Spatial Availability, a Singly-Constrained Measure of Competitive Accessibility
Soukhov, A. and Antonio Paez and Christopher D. Higgins and Moataz Mohamed. PLOS ONE. Repo · Article

Introduces a singly-constrained accessibility measure that avoids multi-counting and yields interpretable, comparable units.

title: Publications
text:
2022

Occupancy and GHG Emissions: Thresholds for Disruptive Transportation Modes and Emerging Technologies
Soukhov, A. and Moataz Mohamed. Transportation Research Part D: Transport and Environment. Article

Defines occupancy and carbon-intensity thresholds where electrification and alternative fuels beat incumbents; buses can abate more per passenger-trip than ride-hail.

Electric Mobility Emission Reduction Policies: A Multi-Objective Optimization Assessment Approach
Soukhov, A. and Ahmed Foda and Moataz Mohamed. Energies. Article

Multi-objective optimization shows cost- and GHG-optimal EV policy mixes for Ontario under budget/abatement targets.
